coredata.dc:title: A homogeneous electrochemiluminescence immunoassay platform based on carbon quantum dots and magnetic beads enrichment for detection of thyroglobulin in serum
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Yu
Zheng, Zhen-Ni
Lin, Xin-Hua
Liu, Ai-Lin
Lei, Yun
coredata.dc:description: Considering the high probability of recurrence or metastasis after thyroidectomy, it is meaningful to develop a rapid, sensitive and specific method for monitoring thyrophyma-related biomarkers. In this study, a homogeneous electrochemiluminescence immunoassay (HO-ECLIA) coupled with magnetic beads (MBs)-based enrichment tactic was established for the determination of thyrophyma-related thyroglobulin (Tg). Importantly, owing to the abundant surface groups and good biocompatibility of carbon quantum dots (CQDs), the incorporation of CQDs onto the Tg antigen surface was achieved, resulting in the formation of Tg-encapsulated CQDs (CQDs-Tg), which served not only as an ECL probe but as a biorecognition element. Under optimal experimental conditions, the proposed platform demonstrated a wide linear range from 0.01 to 100 ng·mL−1 with a detection limit of 6.9 pg·mL−1 (S/N = 3), and performed well in real serum sample analysis against interference. Collectively, the proposed platform exhibited the rapid response, satisfactory sensitivity and specificity toward Tg in complex serum milieu, and held a considerable potential for clinical prognosis monitoring of thyrophyma.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Thyroglobulin
Electrochemiluminescence immunoassay
Carbon quantum dots
Magnetic beads
Homogeneous

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of the HO-ECLIA platform construction process
Experimental optimization of the HO-ECLIA platform
Specificity, reproducibility and stability of the HO-ECLIA platform
Detection of Tg in human serum

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: HRTEM (A), lattice fringe (B), ECL and FL spectra (C), XPS spectrum (D), C1s XPS spectrum (E) and O1s XPS spectrum (F) of CQDs.

CV curves (A) and ECL curves (B) of MGCE in PBS (a), PBS + CQDs (b), PBS + K2S2O8 (c) and PBS + CQDs + K2S2O8 (d). ECL intensity-potential curves (C) and EIS curves (D) of bare MGCE (a), MGCE/AuNPs (b), MGCE/AuNPs/SA-MBs (c), MGCE/AuNPs/SA-MBs/bio-Ab (d), MGCE/AuNPs/SA-MBs/bio-Ab/CQDs-Tg (e), MGCE/AuNPs/SA-MBs/bio-Ab/CQDs-Tg and Tg (f) (scatter is experimental data, solid line is fitted data).
Effect of deposition time of AuNPs (A), concentration of SA-MBs (B), concentration of CQDs-Tg (C) and incubation time (D) on ECL signal.

(A) ECL signals of Tg at different concentrations. (B) Relationship between I and Tg at different concentrations from a to f: 0.01, 0.1, 0.5, 5, 50 and 100 ng·mL−1 (The illustration is the linear relationship between I and the logarithm of Tg concentration). (C) Specificity of the proposed HO-ECLIA platform toward various target proteins. Reproducibility (D), stability of the testing (E) and storage (F) processes of the proposed HO-ECLIA platform.
Schematic illustration of the HO-ECLIA platform based on CQDs and MBs enrichment for the detection of Tg.
The recovery of the ECL immunosensor in human serum (n = 3) (unit: ng·mL−1).

head.ce:title: A homogeneous electrochemiluminescence immunoassay platform based on carbon quantum dots and magnetic beads enrichment for detection of thyroglobulin in serum

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Analysis, Higher Educational Key Laboratory for Nano Biomedical Technology of Fujian Province, Faculty of Pharmacy, Fujian Medical University, Fuzhou, 350122, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Higher Educational Key Laboratory for Nano Biomedical Technology of Fujian Province

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmaceutical Analysis, Higher Educational Key Laboratory for Nano Biomedical Technology of Fujian Province, Faculty of Pharmacy, Fujian Medical University, Fuzhou, 350122, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Considering the high probability of recurrence or metastasis after thyroidectomy, it is meaningful to develop a rapid, sensitive and specific method for monitoring thyrophyma-related biomarkers. In this study, a homogeneous electrochemiluminescence immunoassay (HO-ECLIA) coupled with magnetic beads (MBs)-based enrichment tactic was established for the determination of thyrophyma-related thyroglobulin (Tg). Importantly, owing to the abundant surface groups and good biocompatibility of carbon quantum dots (CQDs), the incorporation of CQDs onto the Tg antigen surface was achieved, resulting in the formation of Tg-encapsulated CQDs (CQDs-Tg), which served not only as an ECL probe but as a biorecognition element. Under optimal experimental conditions, the proposed platform demonstrated a wide linear range from 0.01 to 100 ng·mL−1 with a detection limit of 6.9 pg·mL−1 (S/N = 3), and performed well in real serum sample analysis against interference. Collectively, the proposed platform exhibited the rapid response, satisfactory sensitivity and specificity toward Tg in complex serum milieu, and held a considerable potential for clinical prognosis monitoring of thyrophyma.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Thyroid neoplasms are the most common type of endocrine tumors, with differentiated thyroid cancer (DTC) accounting for over 90% of cases [1]. Typically, patients with thyroid cancer undergo total thyroidectomy, however, 20–40% of patients experience recurrence or metastasis after surgery [2]. Therefore, to detect any recurrence or metastasis as early as possible, lifelong follow-up is recommended, especially long-term DTC-biomarkers monitoring [3]. Thyroglobulin (Tg), a glycoprotein precursor secreted only by thyroid follicular cells, is a crucial biomarker for detecting postoperative tumor recurrence or persistence in patients with DTC [4]. Currently, enzyme linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) and radioimmunoassay (RIA) are the most common methods for Tg detection. However, these methods are inevitably circumscribed by time consumption, tedious operations, the necessity for costly instruments, harmfulness from radioactive isotopes and so forth [5,6]. Accordingly, it is of great importance to develop a simple and sensitive method for Tg analysis to realize prognostic prediction of DTC.

Electrochemiluminescence (ECL), with merits of high sensitivity, fast response, low background and strong maneuverability, exhibits excellent application value in disease diagnosis and prognosis [7]. On account of the superiority of ECL and the salient specificity of competitive immunoassay [8], ECL immunoassay (ECLIA) has been widely used in protein [9,10], virus [11], cell [12] and drug [13] analyses. In ECLIA, numerous ECL probes have been employed, mainly including Ru(bpy)3 2+, luminol, semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) and their analogues [14]. Among them, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have emerged as a popular choice due to their fascinating ECL properties and obvious superiorities, such as high water-solubility, prominent electrical conductivity, minimal cytotoxicity and low cost [15,16]. More importantly, the presence of abundant carboxyl groups on the surface of CQDs enables facile biometric identification, so as to boost the specificity of ECLIA. However, the complicated preparation process of the antibody-tagged ECL probe [17], the tedious stepwise assembly of biomolecules on the electrode [18], the limited modifiable area [19,20] and the steric hindrance of the electrode [21] lead to diminished reaction efficiency and sensitivity, restricting their further development. To this end, homogeneous ECLIA (HO-ECLIA) is a promising alternative to address the above-mentioned issues lying in traditional ECLIA [22,23]. Nevertheless, in a homogeneous system, the presence of residual ECL probes in testing system inevitably leaded to high background signal, which compromises the sensitivity of the biosensor [24]. One major emerging strategy involves the incorporation of magnetic beads (MBs) into HO-ECLIA [25,26]. MBs provide a large surface area, good electrical conductivity, and excellent separation and enrichment performance, which can significantly suppress the ECL background and enhance the detection signal [27,28]. Additionally, MBs can be coated with biomolecules, such as streptavidin, to improve the conjugation of biotinylated antibodies [29], and effectively reduce the complexity and time required for sensing applications [30].

Of note, using an ECL probe to label secondary antibody (Ab2), followed by the construction of a sandwich-type ECLIA platform to detect biomarkers, is a generic and time-consuming way [31–33]. Here, we proposed a competitive HO-ECLIA platform for the effective detection of Tg based on CQDs as luminophores and a magnetic separation and enrichment strategy. As shown in Scheme 1 , the CQDs were synthesized by nitric acid oxidation of carbon fibre. Subsequently, the CQDs were labeled on the surface of Tg antigen, resulting in the formation of the CQDs-Tg nanocomposite (CQDs-Tg), which was adopted as an ECL probe. As a capture probe, the biotinylated Tg antibody (bio-Ab) was bound to the streptavidin-coated MBs (SA-MBs). In addition, Au nanoparticles (AuNPs) were electrodeposited onto the surface of magnetic glassy carbon electrode (MGCE) and served as a bridge to connect the MBs and MGCE, owing its excellent conductivity, which can improve the electron transfer rate, increase the electroactive surface area of the electrodes, and subsequently enhance the ECL signal [34,35]. Based on the principle of competitive immunoassay, when Tg existed in the reaction solution, the target molecule Tg competed with CQDs-Tg to bind the bio-Ab in the homogeneous solution. Due to the limited number of bio-Ab, with the increase of the concentration of Tg, the number of CQDs-Tg that could bind to bio-Ab decreased, leading to a subsequent decrease in ECL intensity. Tg was quantified according to the ECL intensity. The HO-ECLIA could detect Tg with rapid response, high sensitivity and selectivity, thus providing an experimental basis for potential application in clinical prognosis monitoring of DTC.
The materials and apparatus are described in the Supplementary Material.

CQDs were synthesized based on the report with slight changes [36]. Carbon fibre (0.15 g) was added to a nitric acid solution (30 mL, 7.5 M). After heating to 120 °C for 12 h, the mixture cooled down to room temperature naturally and neutralized with NaOH. The resulting solution was filtered by microporous filter membrane (0.22 μm) and ultrafiltered through centrifugal filter devices with 3 kDa molecular weight cut-off (MWCO). Accordingly, the effectively separated fractions with weights equivalent to <3 kDa were collected. The solid product of CQDs was obtained via vacuum-drying at 60 °C.

The CQDs-Tg nanocomposite (CQDs-Tg) was prepared as follows. First, 100 μL CQDs (0.1 mg·mL−1) were mixed with 500 μL of phosphate-buffered saline (PBS, 10 mM, pH 7.4) containing EDC/NHS (12 mg·mL−1) and incubated at 25 °C and 600 rpm for 30 min. Then 100 μL Tg antigen (10 μg·mL−1) was added and incubated for 2 h at 37 °C with shaking (600 rpm). Finally, a micro ultrafiltration tube with a MWCO of 3 kDa was used for ultrafiltration at 4 °C and 11,000 rpm for 0.5 h. The upper layer of the solution >3 kDa was collected to obtain the CQDs-Tg, which was stored at 4 °C for future use.
Briefly, 30 μL of SA-MBs (10 mg·mL−1) were first washed five times with PBS (10 mM, pH 7.4), and resuspended in 400 μL of PBS (10 mM, pH 7.4) to give a 0.75 mg·mL−1 suspension of SA-MBs.

Initially, 25 μL PBS (10 mM, pH 7.4) containing Tg at varied concentrations, 30 μL CQDs-Tg and 45 μL bio-Ab (10 μg·mL−1) were mixed. The mixture was incubated at 37 °C for 30 min. Next, 20 μL SA-MBs suspension (0.75 mg·mL−1) was added and incubated at 37 °C for 10 min. After magnetic separation by a magnetic rack and washing three times with PBS, the trapped nanocomposition was resuspended with 100 μL PBS (10 mM, pH 7.4).

The construction procedure of the HO-ECLIA platform is relatively simple (Scheme 1). Prior to modification, the bare MGCE (Φ = 3 mm) was first polished with a polishing pad and alumina powder (0.3 mm and 0.05 mm). Then, the AuNPs-modified MGCE (MGCE/AuNPs) was prepared via depositing AuNPs by chronoamperometry (i-t) at −0.2 V for 90 s. Subsequently, 5 μL of the nanocomposition solution obtained above was dropped onto MGCE/AuNPs and kept for 3 min, then MGCE/AuNPs/SA-MBs/bio-Ab/CQDs-Tg or Tg was obtained.

The ECL measurement was carried out on a three-electrode system, in which MGCE/AuNPs/SA-MBs/bio-Ab/CQDs-Tg or Tg acted as working electrode, Ag/AgCl worked as reference electrode and platinum electrode served on counter electrode. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) was used to measure the electrochemical response from −1.6 V to 0 V at a scan rate of 0.1 V s−1. To achieve the best results, the measurement was carried out in PBS (10 mM, pH 7.4) containing K2S2O8 (0.1 M) with the photomultiplier tube voltage setting at 800 V. The EIS of the stepwise modification of ECL biosensor was performed in PBS (0.1 M, pH 7.4) including [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− (10 mM) and KCl (0.1 M) with the frequency range from 0.1 Hz to 100,000 Hz.

In order to evaluate the anti-interference ability of the HO-ECLIA platform during actual sample detection, the human serum samples were diluted 10 times prior to use. The samples were then divided into 3 groups, and each group was spiked with different concentrations of Tg standard samples, respectively (denoted as addition). The measurement was repeated three times for each concentration of reaction solution. After measurement, the corresponding Tg concentrations of these samples were obtained.

The synthesis of CQDs was achieved through the oxidation of carbon fibre in nitric acid followed by ultrafiltration. The high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) image of the CQDs (Fig. 1 A) showed their uniformity and good dispersion, with a uniform size of about 3.05 nm. Moreover, the CQDs possessed a good crystal structure. It was observed that the crystal plane spacing between the lattice fringes was 0.21 nm (Fig. 1B), which was basically close to the diffraction facets of graphite carbon (100) [37]. The ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption and fluorescence (FL) spectra of CQDs are shown in Fig. S1A. CQDs had a UV absorption peak at 290 nm, which could be attributed to the n–π* transition of CO on the surface of CQDs. The optimal excitation (λex) and emission (λem) wavelengths were 312 nm and 402 nm, respectively. Compared with the FL spectrum with an emission peak of 402 nm, the ECL spectrum of CQDs shows a maximum emission value at 525 nm (Fig. 1C), and a red shift of 123 nm was observed, this may be attributed to the fact that the energy difference of the surface states responsible for ECL emission of CQDs were lower than their core band gaps for PL emission [38–40]. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was further used to characterize the structure of the product. As seen in Fig. 1D, three peaks of C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s were observed at 284.9, 400.0, and 531.9 eV. In the C 1s spectrum (Fig. 1E), the peaks at 284.8, 286.4, and 288.3 eV, belonged to CC, CO, and O–CO, respectively. Besides, the peaks at 530.5 and 532.3 eV, were observed in the O 1s spectrum (Fig. 1F) and resulted from CO and C–O. The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were depicted in Fig. S1B. The bands of CQDs at 3380, 1600–1800, 1044 and 858 cm−1 were the absorption peaks of O–H stretching vibration, CO stretching vibration, C–O stretching vibration and out-of-plane bending vibration of –OH on the carboxyl group, respectively. The peaks at 1044 and 858 cm−1 on the CQDs decreased, while the C–N stretching vibration peak at 1244 increased. This indicated the successful coupling of the CQDs with Tg due to the amide bond formation.

To exploit the coreactant-type ECL behavior of CQDs using K2S2O8 as the coreactant, both electrochemical technique and ECL analysis were conducted. The CV and ECL properties of MGCE were investigated in a series of test systems. As shown in Fig. 2 A, compared with PBS (10 mM, pH 7.4) (curve a), the CV profile of PBS containing CQDs displayed an additional reduction process, indicating the reduction of CQDs (curve b). When 0.1 M K2S2O8 existed in the 10 mM PBS, the reduction current obviously increased with the peak potential at −1.1 V, attribute to the reduction of S2O8 2− (curve c). Upon the addition of 100 μL of CQDs (0.1 mg·mL−1), the reduction current further increased (curve d). To explore the reason for the strong ECL signal, we measured the ECL curves for these test systems (Fig. 2B). In the control experiment, both of the 10 mM PBS without CQDs (curve a) and the PBS with CQDs (curve b) produced faint ECL signals. In the present of K2S2O8 in PBS solution, there was a weak but observable ECL emission (curve c). When the coreactant of K2S2O8 was introduced, the CQDs presented a significant cathodic ECL response, with onset and peak potentials at −0.8 V and −1.6 V (curve d), respectively. According to the relevant literature [41], the possible ECL enhancement mechanism of the CQDs/S2O8 2− system was discussed. During the negative potential scanning from 0 to −1.6 V, CQDs were reduced to the radical anion of CQDs•− (Eq. (1)), while the coreactant S2O8 2− underwent successive electrochemical reduction and decomposition to form the intermediate radical of SO4 •− (Eq. (2)). Then the reduction product CQDs•− could be excited by SO4 •− to form excited-state CQDs* (Eq. (3)). Eventually, the excited-state species CQDs* were decayed back to the ground state with intense light emission (Eq. (4)).
(1) CQDs + e → CQDs•−
(2) S2O8 2− + e → SO4 •− + SO4 2−
(3) CQDs•− + SO4 •− → CQDs* + SO4 2−
(4) CQDs* → CQDs + hv

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the HO-ECLIA platform construction process
ce:section.ce:para: In order to further investigate the reaction behavior in homogeneous solutions, we demonstrated the construction process of the sensing platform through ECL and EIS characterization after electrode modification. As shown in Fig. 2C, the bare MGCE showed almost no ECL signal (curve a). After AuNPs were deposited on MGCE, the ECL signal was slightly larger (curve b). The ECL signal further increased when MGCE/AuNPs electrode surface adsorbed SA-MBs due to the conductive effect of SA-MBs (curve c) [28]. However, upon coupling SA-MBs with bio-Ab, the ECL signal decreased slightly (curve d), because the electron transfer was hindered by antibody. Conversely, the combination of CQDs-Tg nanocomplex with bio-Ab led to a significant enhancement in the ECL signal (curve e), confirming the successful modification of CQDs to Tg. However, when free Tg competed with CQDs-Tg for bio-Ab, the binding efficiency of CQDs-Tg and bio-Ab was greatly reduced, as free Tg occupied part of bio-Ab, resulting in a significant decrease in the ECL signal (curve f). The ECL responses observed during the various modification steps indicated that the construction of the HO-ECLIA platform was feasible.

In order to better understand the layer assembly process of the modified electrode, the EIS results of the gradual modification of electrode were tested. The recorded Nyquist curves were fitted using Zview software. As shown in Fig. 2D, both MGCE/AuNPs (curve b, 89 Ω) and MGCE/AuNPs/SA-MBs (curve c, 25 Ω) showed a smaller electron-transfer resistance (R et) than bare MGCE (curve a, 244 Ω), which could be attributed to the increasing conductivity caused by AuNPs and SA-MBs modification. When bio-Ab was added onto the electrode surface, the R et subsequently increased (curve d, 2095 Ω), due to electrostatic repulsion between the negatively charged Ab and [Fe(CN)6]3−/4−. However, when CQDs were labeled onto Tg, the R et decreased because of excellent conductivity of CQDs (curve e, 846 Ω). Afterwards, when free Tg competed with CQDs-Tg for bio-Ab, the amount of CQDs-Tg decreased and the R et increased significantly (curve f, 3424 Ω). The EIS results were basically consistent with the ECL results, indicating that the proposed HO-ECLIA platform has been successfully constructed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental optimization of the HO-ECLIA platform
ce:section.ce:para: To achieve excellent analytical performance, some experimental conditions were optimized, including the deposition time of AuNPs, the concentration of SA-MBs, the concentration of CQDs-Tg and incubation time of bio-Ab and CQDs-Tg.

The surface modification of AuNPs on MGCE can increase the electron transfer and improve the sensitivity of the sensor. Chronoamperometry (i-t) was employed to deposit AuNPs at −0.2 V for 30, 60, 90, 120, 150, and 180 s, respectively (Fig. 3 A). As the number of scanning time increased, more and more AuNPs appeared, resulting in closer nanoparticle distances, higher coverage [42], and an increase in the ECL signal. However, when the scanning time exceeded 90 s, an excessive amount of AuNPs induced aggregation, leading to a reduction in the surface area available for anchoring SA-MBs [43]. Consequently, the ECL signal decreased inversely. Therefore, 90 s was chosen as the optimal deposition time.

SA-MBs not only plays the role of a solid-phase carrier, but also can improve electron transfer efficiency due to its electrical conductivity [44]. Therefore, the concentration of SA-MBs was optimized in this experiment. In Fig. 3B, with the augmentation of SA-MBs concentration, the ECL signal intensity increased accordingly. However, when the concentration of SA-MBs was 0.75 mg·mL−1, the ECL signal reached the maximum. Further increments in the SA-MBs concentration resulted in a decline in the ECL signal, because the electrode surface adsorbed multiple layers of SA-MBs, thereby shielding the emission of ECL to the photomultiplier tube detector [45], creating steric hindrance that hindered its binding to bio-Ab, and resulting in a decrease in ECL peak intensity. Therefore, a concentration of 0.75 mg·mL−1 SA-MBs was selected for experimental research.

Since the constructed biosensor adopted the competitive method to detect Tg, the concentration of CQDs-Tg directly affects the experimental results. As shown in Fig. 3C, the ECL intensity reached its maximum at 10 μg·mL−1. Continuing to increase the CQDs-Tg concentration, the ECL intensity remained basically unchanged. In order to save the experimental cost, we chose the CQDs-Tg concentration of 10 μg·mL−1 for experimental research.

The process of antigen-antibody binding in immunoassay required a specific duration to reach dynamic equilibrium. The incubation time had an important influence on the formation of the final SA-MBs/bio-Ab/CQDs-Tg immunocomplex. Among these factors, the incubation of bio-Ab and CQDs-Tg is the most critical, so we studied the impact of incubation time of bio-Ab and CQDs-Tg on ECL signal. Specifically, 45 μL of bio-Ab (10 μg·mL−1) and 30 μL of CQDs-Tg (10 μg·mL−1) were incubated in a centrifugal tube (200 rpm, 37 °C) for 10, 20, 30, 40 and 50 min, respectively. As shown in Fig. 3D,as the incubation time increased, the ECL signal intensity enhanced. When the incubation time reached 30 min, the ECL intensity reached the maximum and exhibited minimal variation with further prolongation of the incubation time, indicating that the binding between antigen and antibody had achieved saturation and stability. Consequently, a reaction time of 30 min was used for the optimal time.

Under the optimized experimental conditions, the analytical performance of the HO-ECLIA platform was evaluated by ECL determination of different concentrations of Tg. As depicted in Fig. 4 A, the ECL intensity showed a decreasing trend with the increase of the concentration of Tg spanned from 0.01 to 100 ng·mL−1. Notably, the concentration of Tg is inversely proportional to the ECL intensity (Fig. 4B), and the linear regression equation of was I = 1363.7 – 531.9lgC (ng·mL−1), R 2 = 0.997, with a limit of detection (LOD) of 6.9 pg·mL−1 (S/N = 3, concentration at which the detected signal value is greater than 3 times the standard deviation of the blank), enough for clinical testing [46,47]. In addition, compared to other Tg detection methods in Table S1, the HO-ECLIA platform displayed lower LOD and broader linear range, suggesting its prospect in disease-related Tg determination.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Specificity, reproducibility and stability of the HO-ECLIA platform
ce:section.ce:para: The specificity of the designed biosensor was studied by introducing different interfering biomolecules, including tumor necrosis factor-α (TNF-α), alpha-fetoprotein (AFP), Osteoitin Cy211 and P-glycoprotein (P-gP). Under optimal conditions, when bio-Ab was incubated with CQDs-Tg (10 μg·mL−1), the ECL intensity was recorded as I 1. When bio-Ab and CQDs-Tg (10 μg·mL−1) were incubated with Tg (5 ng·mL−1) or other tumor markers (0.1 μg·mL−1), the ECL intensity was recorded as I 2. The difference in intensity, ΔI, was calculated by I 1 value minus I 2 value (ΔI = I 1 – I 2). As shown in Fig. 4C, the measured ΔI value obtained after incubating bio-Ab and CQDs-Tg with Tg was significantly higher than other interfering proteins, indicating the excellent specificity of the biosensor. In addition, to assess inter-group reproducibility, the test was repeated 5 times using five distinct biosensors for the solution containing the same concentration of Tg (0.1 ng·mL−1), and the corresponding changes in ECL intensity were observed. The calculated relative standard deviation (RSD) of the five biosensors was 3.25% (Fig. 4D). Hence, the proposed HO-ECLIA platform demonstrated satisfactory reproducibility. The stability assessment of the testing processes was shown in Fig. 4E, when the concentration of Tg was 0.5 ng·mL−1 and 5 ng·mL−1, the ECL intensities of 10 consecutive scans were measured with RSDs of 3.9% and 3.8%, respectively. Finally, we examined the storage time stability of the HO-ECLIA platform (Fig. 4F), and when the Tg concentration was 50 ng·mL−1, the ECL intensity remained relatively stable as the number of days increased. Hence, the HO-ECLIA platform we constructed has good stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Tg in human serum
ce:section.ce:para: Human serum is a complex biological fluid, and a sensor's assay capacity in a complex environment is effectively reflected in the detection tolerance in serum. To minimize potential interference from other serum components, a 10-fold dilution of normal human serum was employed for recovery analysis [48]. The efficacy of the designed HO-ECLIA platform in real serum samples was assessed by adding different concentrations of Tg to a 10-fold diluted normal human serum and conducting ECL measurements three times. The result, as presented in Table 1 , demonstrated the recoveries ranging from 99.5% to 104%, indicating the biosensor possessed excellent potential in the clinical detection of Tg.

In brief, a HO-ECLIA platform was developed based on CQDs, SA-MBs and competitive immunity to achieve sensitive determination of Tg. Specifically, CQDs with favorable biocompatibility was employed to form CQDs-Tg nanocomposites, which not only acted as a biorecognition element, but also functioned as an ECL probe. Meanwhile, SA-MBs provided a high surface area and excellent separation and enrichment capabilities, significantly improving the sensitivity of the HO-ECLIA platform. Ultimately, the detection limit of the prepared HO-ECLIA platform for Tg analysis was downgrade to 6.9 pg·mL−1 (S/N = 3), with a broad detection range of 0.01–100 ng·mL−1. To sum up, the HO-ECLIA platform has remarkable specificity and reproducibility, which is expected to be further exploited for the detection of Tg in early clinical diagnosis and long-term monitoring.

Yu Zhang: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis. Zhen-Ni Zheng: Writing – original draft, Investigation. Xin-Hua Lin: Supervision, Project administration. Ai-Lin Liu: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision. Yun Lei: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (82072379), Joint Funds for the Innovation of Science and Technology, Fujian Province (2020Y9009 & 2019Y9005) and the Natural Science Foundation of Fujian Province (2023J01551).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.talanta.2024.126205.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Management of differentiated thyroid cancer: the standard of care
J. Nucl. Med.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A.M. Avram, K. Zukotynski, H.R. Nadel, L. Giovanella, Management of differentiated thyroid cancer: The standard of care, J. Nucl. Med. 63 (2022) 189-195.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Distinguishing synchronous from metachronous manifestation of distant metastases: a prognostic feature in differentiated thyroid carcinoma
Eur. J. Nucl. Med. Mol. Imag.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Sabet, I. Binse, S. Dogan, A. Koch, S.J. Rosenbaum-Krumme, H-J. Biersack, K. Biermann, S.
